reviewNotes: The five reviewed primary relations are classified exactly once: one two-edge shared stem and three one-edge transcription branches.
The two Douce branches share fol. 15r at the exact Pety Job/Bird transition.
The General Corpus artifact-to-U-M copied_from edge records supported provenance only; the dark U-M raw package prevents any byte-for-byte or canonical-equality claim.
All other witnesses and modern editions are supporting relationships, not ancestors of Kail's 1904 text unless an explicit relation says otherwise.
Printed-volume scans are not manuscript facsimiles.
Complete IIIF bundles are complete only relative to the provider-published canvas inventory, not automatically every physical codex surface.
The Trinity 99-opening O.9.38 object and the distinct 196-canvas CUDL/Scriptorium object retain separate identities, inventories, target locators, access records, and rights statements.
Public access, copyright/license assertions, repository contract terms, and publication permission are separate dimensions.
No exact manuscript text receives a global public-domain inference merely from medieval date.

lineage.summary: The pinned General Corpus XML is a supported repository copy of University of Michigan encoding APE9594.0001.001, which encodes Josef Kail's 1904 EETS Part I selection. Kail transcribes Digby 102 for works 1-24 and Douce 322 separately for Pety Job and The Bird with Four Feathers. The shared artifact-to-encoding-to-edition stem is classified once, followed by three separately scoped transcription branches. Modern editions, collateral witnesses, catalog descriptions, print scans, manuscript-image objects, request routes, and rights statements remain supporting layers.

catalogSummary.title: Twenty-Six Political and Other Poems (Including 'Petty Job') from the Oxford MSS. Digby 102 and Douce 322

catalogingSubject.label: Twenty-Six Political and Other Poems (Including 'Petty Job') from the Oxford MSS. Digby 102 and Douce 322
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Josef Kail's edition-defined twenty-six-work selection as encoded in APE9594.0001.001: all twenty-six poem divisions in the 1904 EETS Original Series 124 volume labeled Part I, printed pp. 1-149. Most front matter and the glossary/back matter survive only as page milestones and are not part of the encoded textual selection. This record does not describe one medieval poem, one authorial anthology, either whole manuscript, or a verified complete projected multi-part publication.

contentParts.label: I. Loue god, and drede.
notes: Kail supplies A.D. 1400; treat as an edition-layer dating claim.
contentParts.summary: Political and moral counsel to governors on justice, charity, self-knowledge, and the fear and love of God.

contentParts.label: II. Mede and muche thank.
notes: Dialogue speakers must not be mistaken for named authors.
contentParts.summary: A debate contrasting true service and earned thanks with flattery, patronage, and material reward.

contentParts.label: III. Treuth, reste, and pes.
notes: Kail supplies early 1401; retain as an edition-layer claim.
contentParts.summary: Political and ethical counsel urging truth, stability, and peace amid failures of governance.

contentParts.label: IV. Lerne say wele, say litel, or say noȝt.
notes: Exact XML heading retained.
contentParts.summary: Didactic counsel on prudent speech, restraint, and the moral and political dangers of ill-advised words.

contentParts.label: V. Wyt & Wille.
notes: Ampersand is present in the encoded heading.
contentParts.summary: An ethical treatment of reason and will, counseling resistance to corrupt desire and bad counsel.

contentParts.label: VI. To lyue bodyly, is perylous.
notes: The XML heading contains an intrusive OCR/read-reference string; the shortened display label is supplied.
contentParts.summary: A moral exhortation contrasting perilous bodily and worldly living with spiritually governed conduct.

contentParts.label: VII. Man, know thy self, and lerne to dye.
notes: DIMEV association is supported by incipit/locus matching; current catalog gives the physical span.
contentParts.summary: A memento-mori exhortation joining self-knowledge, discipline of the soul, and preparation for death.

contentParts.label: VIII. A good makynge of iour delaye.
notes: Kail labels it against the clergy and dates it 1408-09; keep both source-attributed.
contentParts.summary: A topical complaint against clerical delay and failure, framed as counsel toward timely reform.

contentParts.label: IX. With god of loue & pes, ȝe trete.
notes: Kail supplies Easter 1410.
contentParts.summary: An Easter exhortation that links reconciliation with God, moral renewal, love, and public peace.

contentParts.label: X. A good steryng to heuenward.
notes: Fifteen fourteen-line stanzas.
contentParts.summary: Spiritual counsel redirecting the reader from worldly error and presumption toward heaven.

contentParts.label: XI. God & man ben made atte on.
notes: Kail supplies Easter 1412.
contentParts.summary: An Easter devotional poem on Christ's joining of divinity and humanity and the believer's reconciliation with God.

contentParts.label: XII. God saue the kyng, and kepe the croun.
notes: Kail supplies Easter 1413.
contentParts.summary: Prayer and public counsel for the king, crown, common good, and righteous governance.

contentParts.label: XIII. Dede is worchyng.
notes: Kail labels the work A.D. 1414 and in two parts.
contentParts.summary: A two-part political and moral poem that tests professions of good governance against actions and consequences.

contentParts.label: XIV. Man, be warre er the be woo.
notes: Kail supplies early 1418.
contentParts.summary: Admonition that higher rank brings greater responsibility and that prudent reform must precede disaster.

contentParts.label: XV. The descryuyng of mannes membres.
notes: The catalog/DIMEV span is more reliable than the first internal milestone, which occurs after opening lines.
contentParts.summary: A body-part meditation that turns human anatomy into moral instruction and memento mori.

contentParts.label: XVI. A remembraunce of LIJ folyes.
notes: Nine fourteen-line stanzas; Kail supplies A.D. 1419.
contentParts.summary: A catalog of fifty-two follies combining moral rebuke with topical political and social criticism.

contentParts.label: XVII. Loue that god loueth.
notes: Exact title retained from the edition/XML.
contentParts.summary: Instruction in charity and the right ordering of love toward God, neighbor, and virtuous action.

contentParts.label: XVIII. The declaryng of religioun.
notes: Kail supplies A.D. 1421; DIMEV association is incipit/locus supported.
contentParts.summary: A reformist explanation of true religious life and the conduct expected of clergy and professed religious.

contentParts.label: XIX. God's Appeal to Man.
notes: The XML's internal folio milestone `110a` is an error; the current catalog and sequence establish ff. 119v-120r.
contentParts.summary: A complaint in which God addresses sinful humanity and calls it to recognition, amendment, and gratitude.

contentParts.label: XX. How Man's flesh complained to God against Christ.
notes: Bracketed title is editorially supplied in Kail.
contentParts.summary: A theological complaint and dialogue that dramatizes human embodiment, sin, divine justice, and Christ's claims upon humanity.

contentParts.label: XXI. A lernyng to good leuynge.
notes: Twenty eight-line stanzas.
contentParts.summary: Practical moral instruction for virtuous living, repentance, charity, and responsibility toward others.

contentParts.label: XXII. Knowe thy self and thy god.
notes: Nine eight-line stanzas.
contentParts.summary: A compact devotional meditation joining self-knowledge, knowledge of God, humility, and salvation.

contentParts.label: XXIII. Of the sacrament of the Altere.
notes: Sixteen eight-line stanzas.
contentParts.summary: A Eucharistic devotional poem on Christ's presence in the sacrament of the altar and the communicant's response.

contentParts.label: XXIV. The Lessouns of the Dirige.
notes: The edition asserts 52 eight-line stanzas = 416 lines, but raw XML has 418 L elements; ten child lessons are non-counting analytical sections.
contentParts.summary: A Middle English verse paraphrase of the lessons of Matins in the Office of the Dead, organized as nine lessons plus Libera me.

contentParts.label: Lectio prima: Parce mihi, domine.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.
contentParts.summary: First lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.

contentParts.label: Lectio secunda: Tedet animam meam.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.
contentParts.summary: Second lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.

contentParts.label: Lectio tertia: Manus tue fecerunt me.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.
contentParts.summary: Third lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.

contentParts.label: Lectio quarta: Quantas habeo.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.
contentParts.summary: Fourth lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.

contentParts.label: Lectio quinta: Homo natus.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.
contentParts.summary: Fifth lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.

contentParts.label: Lectio sexta: Quis mihi hoc tribuat.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.
contentParts.summary: Sixth lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.

contentParts.label: Lectio septima: Spiritus meus.
notes: Non-counting subdivision; XML records an abbreviation note in the heading.
contentParts.summary: Seventh lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.

contentParts.label: Lectio octava: Pelli mee.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.
contentParts.summary: Eighth lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.

contentParts.label: Lectio nona: Quare de uulua eduxisti me.
notes: Non-counting subdivision; the XML heading records a manuscript-reading note.
contentParts.summary: Ninth lesson section of the Dirige paraphrase.

contentParts.label: Libera me, domine.
notes: Non-counting subdivision.
contentParts.summary: Concluding Libera me section following the nine lessons.

contentParts.label: XXV. Pety Job, or Parce mihi, Domine!
notes: 684 poetic lines plus two prose-like colophon statements encoded as L. Direct f.15r image and Fein/Kail conflict with DIMEV 3075's printed endpoint ff.10ra-15vb. Richard Rolle attribution rejected.
contentParts.summary: A mid-fifteenth-century penitential monologue that expands and paraphrases the nine lessons of the Office of the Dead from Job, with Latin subjects and the recurring plea Parce mihi, domine.

contentParts.label: XXVI. The Bird with Four Feathers.

locators.value: ff. 15r-16v; begins after Pety Job on f. 15r

notes: Common modern title supplied from scholarship; the XML heading reads `Here begynneth a tretyse of Parce michi, domine.` DIMEV gives ff.15rb-16vb.
contentParts.summary: A penitential allegory in which a narrator encounters a plucked bird whose lost feathers are youth, beauty, strength, and riches; variable-length stanzas return to Parce mihi, domine.

extent.description: Thirty-six content-part records comprise twenty-six count-bearing verse works and ten null-count analytical lesson sections nested inside poem XXIV. The XML contains 529 LG elements and 4,696 raw L elements; the verse-line total is 4,694 after excluding two prose-like Pety Job colophon statements encoded as L. Poem XXIV has 418 L elements against the edition's idealized 52 eight-line stanzas/416 lines, Pety Job has 684 poetic lines plus the two colophon statements, and the Bird poem has 240 raw lines with an encoded note that print line number 222 repeats after 223.

formStatements.description: All twenty-six count-once works are verse. Two prose-like Pety Job colophon statements are encoded as L elements but do not make the edited selection mixed-form.

assertion.notes: The first twenty-three Digby poems contain no Latin according to Verheij. Modern English editorial apparatus is not a work-level discovery language.

scope.description: Latin lesson headings, subjects, liturgical phrases, and/or recurring refrains intrinsic to parts 24-26.

notes: Catalog the edition-defined twenty-six-work selection, not one poem or either whole codex.
Count each top-level poem once; the ten nested lessons of poem XXIV are analytical sections with null item counts.
Keep modern English editorial prose outside work-level language facets; expose Latin only for parts 24-26.
Treat Digby 102's Malvern-area dialect as a witness-level scribal localization, not a composition place.
Keep the Richard Rolle witness-rubric attribution as rejected attribution history.
Retain Part I on the edition layer without claiming a published Part II or a complete projected multi-part edition.
Preserve the DIMEV 3075 Douce endpoint conflict and the exact shared f. 15r seam between Pety Job and The Bird with Four Feathers.
Use 4,694 as the audited poetic-line total while retaining 4,696 raw XML L elements and both Pety Job colophon statements.
Do not transfer manuscript production dates, edition publication place, or holding locations into work-composition claims.

summaries.value: Josef Kail's 1904 edition-defined selection of twenty-six anonymous Middle English verse works: twenty-four political, moral, and devotional poems from Bodleian Library MS Digby 102, followed by Pety Job and The Bird with Four Feathers from MS Douce 322. The final three works draw on the Office of the Dead and Job, while the wider Digby sequence ranges across governance, reform, ethical counsel, Eucharistic devotion, death, and salvation.

The record counts the twenty-six separately headed poems once. The ten lessons nested inside poem XXIV are non-counting analytical sections. The 1904 title-page Part I wording is retained, but neither a published Part II nor the projected publication's completion or abandonment has been verified. Modern editorial prose and apparatus remain edition-layer material rather than additional medieval works or work-level English-language facets.

work.summary: Josef Kail's 1904 edition-defined selection of twenty-six anonymous Middle English verse works: twenty-four political, moral, and devotional poems from Bodleian Library MS Digby 102, followed by Pety Job and The Bird with Four Feathers from MS Douce 322. The final three works draw on the Office of the Dead and Job, while the wider Digby sequence ranges across governance, reform, ethical counsel, Eucharistic devotion, death, and salvation.
